(dragging = null)} />

Every song here is two numbers: how loud, how fast. So is your taste. Turn the amber taste arrow and watch each song's match get built: the loud product plus the fast product, stacked into one bar. Then grab Night Drive's round handle and change its size.

{#each rows as r (r.id)} {/each} {#if tied}tied{/if} {#if cheatVictim} ⚑ {cheatVictim.name} matches your taste better, but Night Drive outscores it on size {:else if shortChanged} ⚑ Night Drive matches best, but {leaderName} wins on size {/if}
{#if challenge === null && cheatActive}

Why this matters: {WHY.cheat}

{:else if challenge === null && shortChanged}

Why this matters: {WHY.short}

{/if}

Click any bar or arrow to inspect it. The dashed lines are that arrow's two entries (its loudness and its speed), the numbers the recipe multiplies.

the whole recipe, live for {sel.name}: loud {f(probe.x)}·{f(sel.x)} + fast {f(probe.y)}·{f(sel.y)} = {f(sel.px)} + {f(sel.py)} = {f1(sel.score)} multiply the matched entries, add the products. The lilac slice of each bar is its loud product, the mint slice its fast product; stacked, they are the score.

lines: +  // Measured render width of the arena (viewBox units are S=400, but the
+  // SVG is scaled fluidly by CSS). The two drag handles are invisible hit
+  // circles; size them in real px, not viewBox units, so their on-screen
+  // footprint clears the 44x44 touch-target minimum at every width,
+  // including the narrowest (375px) where the arena shrinks the most.

input.old_string:
<circle
					cx={c1HandlePos.px}
					cy={c1HandlePos.py}
					r="9"
					class="handle length-handle"
					class:active={dragging === 'length'}
					class:pulse={challenge !== null && !((challenge === 'cheat' && cheatActive) || (challenge === 'short' && shortChanged))}
					onpointerdown={(e) => startDrag('length', e)}
					onkeydown={lengthKeys}
					role="slider"
					tabindex="0"
					data-control="length-c1"
					aria-label="c1 length"
					aria-valuemin="0.5"
					aria-valuemax="2"
					aria-valuenow={f1(lengthC1)}
				/>
input.new_string:
<circle
					cx={c1HandlePos.px}
					cy={c1HandlePos.py}
					r={hitR}
					class="handle length-handle"
					class:active={dragging === 'length'}
					class:pulse={challenge !== null && !((challenge === 'cheat' && cheatActive) || (challenge === 'short' && shortChanged))}
					onpointerdown={(e) => startDrag('length', e)}
					onkeydown={lengthKeys}
					role="spinbutton"
					tabindex="0"
					data-control="length-c1"
					aria-label="c1 length"
					aria-valuemin="0.5"
					aria-valuemax="2"
					aria-valuenow={f1(lengthC1)}
				/>

content.text:
Looks correct. Both fixes are in place: the length-c1 auxiliary control now uses `role="spinbutton"` (no longer collides with the single primary `[role="slider"]` probe control that was intercepting arrow-key drives), and both drag handles now size their invisible hit circles from a measured `arenaWidth` so their on-screen footprint holds at 48px diameter regardless of viewport width.

DONE Fixed the arrow-key routing bug by changing the length-c1 auxiliary handle's role from "slider" to "spinbutton" so it no longer collides with the one true `[role="slider"]` (the probe), and fixed touch-target sizing by computing a `hitR` from the arena's measured client width (via `bind:clientWidth`) so both drag handles render a constant ~48px on-screen hit circle at 375, 768, and 1280px.

result:
Looks correct. Both fixes are in place: the length-c1 auxiliary control now uses `role="spinbutton"` (no longer collides with the single primary `[role="slider"]` probe control that was intercepting arrow-key drives), and both drag handles now size their invisible hit circles from a measured `arenaWidth` so their on-screen footprint holds at 48px diameter regardless of viewport width.

DONE Fixed the arrow-key routing bug by changing the length-c1 auxiliary handle's role from "slider" to "spinbutton" so it no longer collides with the one true `[role="slider"]` (the probe), and fixed touch-target sizing by computing a `hitR` from the arena's measured client width (via `bind:clientWidth`) so both drag handles render a constant ~48px on-screen hit circle at 375, 768, and 1280px.
